title: KMS & data protection
summary: A team set up an "encrypted" S3 export bucket — but the protection is theatre. Scope an over-privileged analyst off the key and enforce encryption so the data is actually protected.
learningObjectives: Understand that KMS access is governed by BOTH the key policy and IAM — and why kms:* on Resource '*' is dangerous (it includes ScheduleKeyDeletion and DisableKey).
Scope a principal to least privilege on a specific key (kms:Decrypt on one key ARN, not kms:* on every key).
Enforce encryption at rest by denying S3 uploads that don't use the intended KMS key.
Enforce encryption in transit by denying non-TLS (aws:SecureTransport=false) requests.

successCriteria.description: The /lab/data-analyst user no longer has kms:* on Resource '*' — scoped to kms:Decrypt on the lab key only.
successCriteria.verify: No policy on /lab/data-analyst grants kms:* (or kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion / kms:DisableKey / kms:PutKeyPolicy) on Resource '*'.

successCriteria.description: The exports bucket denies PutObject that isn't SSE-KMS with the lab key, and denies non-TLS requests.
successCriteria.verify: Bucket policy on sslab-kms-exports-<acct> Denies s3:PutObject when s3:x-amz-server-side-encryption != aws:kms (or the wrong key), AND Denies requests where aws:SecureTransport = false.

hints: KMS authorization is the key policy AND IAM together. The key policy here is the safe root-admin default — the problem is the analyst's IAM policy granting kms:* on every key.
kms:* includes kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion and kms:DisableKey — an 'analyst' should never hold those. Replace it with kms:Decrypt scoped to the single lab key ARN.
Default bucket encryption is not ENFORCED encryption — a client can override it on upload. Add a bucket policy that Denies s3:PutObject unless s3:x-amz-server-side-encryption equals aws:kms.
Add the classic Deny on aws:SecureTransport=false so nothing moves in plaintext over the wire.
